Question 25:
The Company has various grievance redressal processes for the stakeholders. Details are as:
Investors and Shareholders: The Company has established a dedicated grievance redressal mechanism through IGRC@larsentoubro.com, enabling investors and shareholders to raise concerns in a structured and accessible manner. In addition, multiple channels are available for submission of grievances, including: a) Physical letters addressed to the Company b) E-mails to the Registrar and Transfer Agent (RTA), KFin Technologies Ltd. (KFintech), at einward.ris@kfintech.com c) Telephone calls or in-person visits to registered office of Company in Mumbai, RTA office in Hyderabad d) SCORES: SEBI’s online grievance redressal platform e) SmartODR: Securities Market Approach for Resolution through Online Dispute Resolution Grievances received through the IGRC e-mail are addressed promptly where requisite details are available. Complaints received by the RTA are forwarded to the Company, with scanned records accessible through the Karisma system (KFintech portal). The Company regularly monitors the Inward Report on the Karisma portal to ensure adherence to the defined Service Level Agreement (SLA) timelines of 30 days for resolution of queries and complaints. On a quarterly basis, the Company discloses to the Stock Exchanges the number of complaints received and resolved. These reports are also reviewed by the Stakeholders’ Relationship Committee and presented to the Board, ensuring appropriate oversight and governance.
Supply chain partners: The Company has dedicated vendor management platform, Partner Portal, across business verticals to capture and address grievances from supply chain partners. The platform enables structured handling of issues related to contractual matters, including administrative and statutory compliances, payments and invoicing, contractual terms, scheduling and delivery of materials and services and quality non-conformances. The typical resolution timeline for such grievances is 30 - 45 working days, with more complex cases requiring additional time. For grievances beyond contractual matters, such as concerns relating to unethical behaviour, misconduct, violations of legal or regulatory requirements or fraud, the Company has established a Whistleblower Policy for Vendors and Channel Partners. This policy provides a formal mechanism for reporting and addressing such issues and is accessible to all registered vendors across the Company. The policy is publicly available at Corporate Policies. Business Responsibility & sustainability Reporting 10 Integrated Annual Report 2025-26 The grievance redressal mechanisms for employees, contract workers, community and customers are explained in Principle 3, Principle 8 and Principle 9 respectively.

xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DescriptionOfBusinessActivity: Engineering and Construction of (a) Building and Factories, (b) Transportation Infrastructure, (c) Heavy Civil Infrastructure, (d) Power Transmission & Distribution, (e) Renewables, (f) Water & Effluent Treatment and (f) Minerals and Metals

EPC solutions in (a) Hydrocarbon Onshore and Offshore businesses covering oil & gas, refineries, petrochemicals & offshore wind energy sectors, from frontend design through detailed engineering, modular fabrication, procurement, project management, construction, installation and commissioning, and (b) CarbonLite Solutions business covering BTG scope for power generation plants including associated systems and/or carbon capture utilisation & utility packages

Design, manufacture / construct, supply, revamp/retrofit of (a) Heavy Engineering business covering custom designed, engineered critical equipment & systems to the process plant and nuclear energy (b) Precision Engineering & Systems covering marine and land platforms including related equipment & systems; aerospace products & systems; precision and electronic products & systems for the defence, security, space and industrial sectors
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:ProductOrServiceSoldByTheEntit: Manufacture of auxiliary plant for use with steam generators (condensers, economizers, superheaters, steam collectors and accumulators)
Manufacture of parts and accessories for machinery / equipment used by construction and mining industries
Construction of buildings carried out on own-account basis or on a fee or contract basis
Construction and maintenance of motorways, streets, roads, other vehicular and pedestrian ways,highways, bridges, tunnels and subways; Construction and maintenance of railways and rail-bridges

Construction and maintenance of power plants; Construction / erection and maintenance of power, telecommunication and transmission lines; Construction and maintenance of water main and line connection, water reservoirs including irrigation system (canal); Construction and repair of sewer systems including sewage disposal plants and pumping stations
Construction and maintenance of industrial facilities such as refineries, chemical plants, etc.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:ABriefOnTypesOfCustomersExplan: The Company’s core businesses include EPC projects in infrastructure and energy, along with hi-tech manufacturing of equipment and systems for process plants and other industries. Government (sovereign, sub-national, local) and related entities are the primary customers, contributing ~ 65% of revenue, while the remaining business comes from private sector clients, both domestic and international, across diverse sectors. Approximately 67% of revenue is derived from domestic (India-based) customers.

xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:StatementByDirectorResponsible:
L&T’s commitment to the society and the environment, embedded in our Sustainability Vision ‘For a Better World’, continues to be a fundamental driver of long-term value creation and business resilience. In an increasingly volatile global environment marked by geopolitical uncertainties and supply chain disruptions, commitments to environmental and social priorities are being tested worldwide. However, in alignment with national priorities and our ethos, sustainability remains strategic imperatives for the Company.
As a part of our commitment to environmental stewardship, the Company has set ambitious targets of achieving Carbon Neutrality by 2040 and Water Neutrality by 2035. These goals have been operationalised through structured five-year cycles aligned with business strategy planning under Lakshya. During the first cycle (FY 2020-21 to FY 2025-26), we achieved a 32% reduction in GHG emissions intensity, primarily driven by a corresponding reduction in energy intensity.
While further decarbonisation opportunities remain, particularly through increased adoption of renewable electricity, currently at 19%, the Company is adopting Group Captive renewable power arrangements for key manufacturing facilities and offices. Our businesses continue to focus on targeted initiatives including energy efficiency improvements, adoption of low- and zero-carbon fuels and scaling renewable energy usage.
Progress towards Water Neutrality is being driven through a multi-pronged approach encompassing water use efficiency, wastewater recycling and increased reliance on non-freshwater sources. Rainwater harvesting continues to be a key lever, with enhanced capacity created at major manufacturing locations. Across EPC project sites, wastewater recycling reached 2.7 million kl in FY 2025-26.
In addition, water stewardship efforts are complemented by community-based interventions under our CSR programmes. Independent assessments of water conservation potential across project locations indicate that approximately 4 million kl water were conserved during the year. These efforts contributed to a 25% reduction in net freshwater withdrawal intensity compared to the previous year, reinforcing our path towards Water Neutrality by 2035.
Recognising sustainability as a key business opportunity, L&T developed a portfolio of ‘Green Business’ offerings that enable customers to reduce environmental impact and optimise resource efficiency. This portfolio continues to expand, contributing meaningfully to revenue and positioning the Company to capitalise on emerging low-carbon and sustainable infrastructure opportunities. For FY 2025-26, the ‘Green Business’ portfolio revenue stood at ~R 78,700 Cr., contributing 51% to the Company’s total revenue. L&T’s leadership in offering solutions for a sustainable future has also been recognized by Engineering News-Record (ENR), one of the globally recognised publications in the construction industry and we have enhanced our standing to 2nd from 3rd rank in Top 200 Environmental Firms Survey conducted annually.
On the social front, Health and safety remain paramount. A strengthened Corporate EHS function drives implementation of our safety strategy, ‘VISION KNOW HARM’, focused on proactive risk identification and strict adherence to safety protocols. During FY 2025-26, we have recorded 7.4 million safety training hours and over 3,75,000 contract workers underwent structured health and safety training, reinforcing a strong safety culture across operations.
Corporate Social Responsibility has long been integral to L&T’s values. In FY 2025-26, CSR initiatives positively impacted approximately 1.9 million lives across India. Our flagship programme, Unnati (Integrated Community Development Programme), continues to enhance resilience in water-stressed regions, benefiting over 30,000 households and significantly improving groundwater levels. New initiatives such as Nayi Disha focus on strengthening communities of migrant construction workers, while Project Jyoti is transforming STEM education in government schools through digital infrastructure and experiential learning, improving student outcomes and engagement. The Company is actively scaling these proven models to maximise impact across geographies, contributing to inclusive growth, reduced inequalities and community empowerment.
Beyond programmes, we are strengthening systems, processes and disclosures. We remain committed to aligning withglobal standards, enhancing transparency and integrating sustainability across the value chain, from employee well-beingand circular economy practices to clean energy transition and responsible supply chains.
‘Every great story begins with a leap’ reflects our belief in taking purposeful strides - leveraging technology, raising our ambitions and delivering measurable impact across ESG priorities. As we move forward, we remain focused on creatingenduring value for all our stakeholders while contributing to a more resilient, inclusive, and sustainable future.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfTheHighestAuthorityRe: The Chairman & MD and the Board are the highest authority responsible for implementation and oversight of the Business Responsibility policies.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfSpecifiedCommitteeOfT: The Company’s CSR & Sustainability Committee of the Board is responsible for decision making on sustainability related issues. For details of the composition, role and terms of reference, please refer to Annexure ‘B’ to the Board Report in the Integrated Annual Report FY 2025-26.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NameOfTheAgencyIfTheEntityCarr:
The Company’s policies are translated into action through robust, well defined governance frameworks, processes and management systems. The effectiveness of these systems is subject to regular independent evaluation through third party certifications and audits conducted by internationally recognised agencies. Across its businesses and operational facilities, the Company maintains over 300 global certifications, reflecting conformance with a broad spectrum of internationally accepted standards. These include, among others, ISO/IEC 27001 (Information Security Management Systems), ISO 14001 (Environmental Management Systems), ISO 45001 (Occupational Health and Safety Management Systems), ISO 9001 (Quality Management Systems), SA 8000 (Social Accountability), ISO 29001, ISO 50001 (Energy Management Systems), ISO 20000, ISO 30400 series (Human Capital Management), ISO 56000 series (Innovation Management), ISO 31000 series (Risk Management) and other sector specific and functional standards.
As part of the certification and surveillance process, independent external agencies assess the effectiveness of the Company’s policies and management systems through comprehensive reviews of governance structures, documented procedures, operational controls, performance records and monitoring mechanisms. These assessments provide assurance on compliance with applicable standards, support continuous improvement and reinforce the integrity and effectiveness of the Company’s policy implementation framework.

xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:AntiCorruptionOrAntiBriberyPol:
The Company has in place a comprehensive Anti Bribery and Anti Corruption (ABAC) Policy that provides a structured framework to prevent, identify and address bribery and corruption risks across all its operations. The ABAC Policy explicitly prohibits all forms of bribery and corrupt practices, including facilitation payments, kickbacks, improper inducements and other unlawful or unethical payments, whether direct or indirect. The policy is aligned with applicable Indian laws, including the Prevention of Corruption Act and reflects internationally accepted principles of ethical business conduct.
The policy is applicable to all employees, including Board Members and Senior Management and is extended to associated persons, intermediaries and value chain partners through the Company’s Code of Conduct for Suppliers. Effective implementation of the policy is supported by robust internal controls, risk based monitoring mechanisms, periodic compliance reviews and structured training and awareness programmes.
The anti corruption framework is further strengthened through complementary governance instruments, including the Code of Conduct for Board Members and Employees, the Whistle Blower Policy and the Whistle Blower Policy for Vendors and Channel Partners, which enable confidential reporting of concerns and ensure timely, independent investigation and resolution. The Company maintains a zero tolerance approach to bribery and corruption and is committed to fostering a culture of integrity, transparency and ethical conduct across its operations and value chain. The ABAC Policy is available at Corporate Policies.
For more information on ABAC disclosures, please refer to ‘Sustainability Governance’ chapter of the Integrated Annual Report FY 2025-26.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfAnyCorrectiveActionTa: No cases or complaints received in the above matters.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfTheEntityHaveProcesse:
The Company has established a formal and robust framework to identify, disclose and manage conflicts of interest involving members of the Board. Under this framework, Directors are required to make timely and full disclosures of any actual, potential, or perceived conflicts of interest, including those arising from other directorships, fiduciary responsibilities or business relationships. Where a conflict is identified, the concerned Director is required to recuse himself or herself from deliberations and decision making on the relevant matter, in accordance with applicable laws, governance norms and internal policies.
Board Members are expected to discharge their duties in good faith and in the best interests of the Company, always exercising independent and objective judgment. They are required to avoid any situation that could compromise their impartiality or give rise to undue influence and are prohibited from using their position for personal benefit, whether directly or indirectly, to the detriment of the Company or its stakeholders. Any conflict of interest involving a Board Member is required to be promptly reported to the Chairman of the Audit Committee and/or the Chairman of the Board, as appropriate. These disclosures are overseen through established governance processes to ensure transparency, accountability and adherence to high standards of ethical conduct. This structured approach reinforces the Company’scommitment to sound corporate governance and effective Board oversight.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NotesPrinciple1ExplanatoryText:
Partners are selected based on their criticality to business operations, scale of engagement and potential environmental and social risks. In line with the nine Principles of the National Guidelines for Responsible Business Conduct (NGRBC), the Company undertakes structured programmes to enhance awareness and strengthen responsible practices across its supply chain, including contractors and subcontractors. Key focus areas during the year include:
Safety: The Company continues to place the highest emphasis on safety across all project sites and manufacturinglocations. Regular training and sensitisation sessions are conducted for employees, contractual workers andsubcontractors, covering: a) Safety induction and hazard identification b) Appropriate use of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) c) Occupational health and hygiene practices d) Emergency preparedness and response e) Job-specific safety protocols, including working at heights, excavation safety, tunnelling safety and hot work procedures f) To institutionalise a strong safety culture, it is mandatory for all individuals-including employees, contractor workforce, vendor personnel, customers and visitors-to undergo safety induction prior to commencing any activity at project sites or manufacturing facilities.
Sustainability and Responsible Business Practices: During FY 2025-26, the Company scaled up the sustainability awareness among its supply chain partners. Key initiatives include: . Conducted two structured virtual training sessions for 180 critical supply chain partners . Conducted in person training session for Middle East supply chain partners . Undertook ESG assessments for 169 critical supply chain partners through an independent third-party agency .Organised multiple handholding and guidance sessions to support partners in understanding assessment requirements, improving disclosures and strengthening their ESG performance
These initiatives are aimed at embedding responsible business conduct across the value chain, enhancing transparency and enabling supply chain partners to align with evolving regulatory and stakeholder expectations. For further details, refer to ‘Social and Relationship Capital’ of the Integrated Annual Report FY 2025-26.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:TopicsOrPrinciplesCoveredUnder: Basics of sustainability, regulatory landscape including global & Indian including National Guidelines on Responsible Business Conduct (NGRBC) Principles and SEBI’s BRSR Core, L&T’s policies and code of conduct, health and safety, human rights, environment parameters, ESG assessment being conducted by the Company and other good practices on ESG
In-depth training sessions carried out by a third party on governance, ethical business practices, human rights and labour management, healthand safety and environment
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfImprovementsInEnviron: A few initiatives, but not limited to, stated below: Replacing old equipment with higher energy efficiency or productivity equipment; Installation of rooftop solar systems; Installation of solar powered equipment e.g., light masts; Installation of sewage treatment plant, effluent treatment plant for wastewater treatment and recycling; Installation of water flowmeters and smart meters; Purchase of bio-digestor for recycling canteen waste; Spend on facilities for skill training institutes

Essential Indicators: Q1 R&D 2.7% [R 4.8 Cr] 5.4% [R 9.5 Cr] Capex 2.7% [R 69.9 Cr] 2.8% [R 42.6 Cr]
Q2 Does the entity have procedures in place for sustainable sourcing? (Yes/No) Yes. The Company has established procedures for sustainable sourcing as an integral part of its commitment to responsible and ethical business practices. Sustainability considerations are systematically embedded across procurement processes and supplier engagement frameworks, recognising the material environmental, social and governance impacts associated with the supply chain. L&T expects its suppliers and business partners to adopt appropriate policies, systems and management practices in line with the Company’s Sustainable Supply Chain Policy and Code of Conduct for Suppliers. These frameworks set out clear expectations on ethical conduct, environmental stewardship, respect for human rights, health and safety and regulatory compliance.
Through these procedures, the Company promotes responsible sourcing practices, encourages continuous improvement in ESG performance across the value chain and seeks to build long term, resilient relationships with suppliers that are aligned with its sustainability objectives.
Key Principles: .Environmental Responsibility: Efficient use of resources, conservation of energy and water, increased use of recycled materials and adoption of a precautionary approach to environmental management. .Human Rights and Labour Practices: Compliance with health and safety standards (aligned with ISO 45001), timely payment of wages and strict prohibition of child and forced labour. . Ethical Business Practices: Adherence to anti-corruption principles and responsible business conduct.
Key Sustainable Sourcing Practices: .A Sustainable Supply Chain Policy that defines expectations on environmental protection, occupational health and safety, labour standards, human rights, ethical conduct and governance. . Supplier Code of Conduct applicable to all vendors, covering ESG requirements including ethics, human rights and environmental compliance. . Capacity building and handholding of critical suppliers to improve ESG performance. . Supplier sustainability assessments conducted annually for select critical suppliers by an independent third party, with both online and onsite audits. . Training and awareness programme conducted for more than 1,200 Tier-I supply chain partners in the last three years.
Promotion of low-carbon and recycled materials, including use of steel produced through electric arc/ induction furnace routes and blended cement in place of Ordinary Portland Cement, wherever feasible and aligned with technical specifications.
If yes, what percentage of inputs were sourced sustainably? Accounts for ~20% of procurement value, based on sourcing from 210 ‘Green’-rated critical supply chain partners under the Company’s sustainability assessment programme, conducted by an independent third-party agency.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:IndicateInPutMaterial: Fly ash and Ground Granulated Blast-furnace Slag (GGBS) in place of Cement
Fly ash and Ground Granulated Blast-furnace Slag (GGBS) in place of Cement
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfMechanismAvailableToR: Registration and redressal are done through worker union or Joint Management Council or Complaints and Grievance Committee.

Contractual workers, over 3,75,000 in number for FY 2025-26, are recognized as critical yet vulnerable stakeholders by the Company. The Company has established structured and accessible grievance redressal mechanisms to ensure that their concerns are addressed in a fair, transparent and timely manner. Contractual workers may raise grievances individually or collectively, either directly or through designated worker representatives. These grievances may relate to working conditions, living conditions at labour colonies, wage-related matters, or any other employment-related concerns.
Manufacturing Facilities: Contract labour management cells and Industrial Relations (IR) departments are responsible for overseeing the well-being of contractual workers. Key elements of the framework include: Periodic interactions between IR teams, contractors and contractual workers to understand and mitigate their grievances; Records are maintained and periodically reviewed by the senior management. Formation of worker representative committees to ensure fair representation, which regularly engage with management to raise and resolve issues; Certification of select facilities to SA8000 standards, reinforcing adherence to internationally recognised labour and human rights practices
EPC Project Sites For EPC project sites, a formalised grievance redressal system is implemented across locations through grievance redressal personnel/teams. The grievance redressal process includes the following steps: Registration of grievance (oral, written, or via toll-free helpline); Investigation and assessment; Resolution within defined timelines; Escalation, where required; Feedback collection and closure; Record-keeping, reporting and periodic review and audit; Additionally, a 24x7 toll-free helpline is available to facilitate grievance registration, further enhancing accessibility for workers.

The Company has established a digital platform, HEERA, as the primary channel for managing employee grievances. Employees may raise grievances related to work environment, working relationships, workplace conditions, salary and compensation, claims settlement, reimbursement and recovery of dues, leave management, medical insurance and health insurance or any other matter impacting employment and working environment.
Employees are encouraged to initially seek grievance resolution through discussions with their Immediate Supervisor. In the absence of an Immediate Supervisor, grievances may be escalated to the next level supervisor. If satisfactory resolution is not achieved, employees may register their grievance through the HEERA chatbot, creating a grievance ticket for formal resolution. Designated HR Officers are responsible for resolving the grievances. Depending on the severity and complexity of the grievance, resolution timelines and escalation procedures are defined to ensure timely and effective redressal.
Grievances are submitted to respective HR coordinators/partners who are responsible for resolution.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfOccupationalHealthAnd:
The Company’s Environment, Health & Safety (EHS) Policy and ‘VISION KNOW HARM’ extend beyond management system implementation and focus on ensuring the well-being of all personnel. All work locations of the Company, including EPC project sites, manufacturing facilities and offices, are covered under the Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) Management System of the business operating at those locations. The OHS system covers all employees and contractors, including non-permanent employees and contractual workers. The OHS Management System, internally referred to as the EHS Management System, is implemented at the project and business levels and is established based on the ISO 45001:2018 standard. This provides a structured framework for the assessment and management of OHS/EHS risks and supports continuous improvement in safety performance.
In addition to internal assurance processes, the effectiveness of the OHS/EHS Management System is validated through certification by third-party agencies and it is certified with applicable standards, including ISO 45001:2018. For further details on safety management system, refer to ‘Human Capital’ section of the Integrated Annual Report FY 2025-26.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DesclosureOfTheProcessesUsedTo:
The EHS Management System has various processes to support the identification of hazards and the assessment of associated risks. Two types of risk assessments are carried out based on the sector and the nature of activities at specific work locations. Risk assessments include Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA), Job Hazard Analysis (JHA), Hazard Identification (HAZID), Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP), Failure Modes, Effects and Criticality Analysis (FMECA) and other Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) methods. The risk assessment process actively involves relevant stakeholders, including design engineers, construction engineers, planning functions, EHS team members and other personnel working at the location, including contractual workers, to ensure comprehensive risk assessment and the design and implementation of effective mitigation plans.
Operational risks are identified and managed through an internal process named the ‘Daily Safety Cycle’. It includes tasks that need to be carried out before and during work on a daily basis to mitigate risks. Digital platforms and mobile applications have also been implemented for effective execution and tracking. In FY 2025–26, the Company initiated additional measures, such as Take-5, Hazard Hunt and Rule of Three, to further strengthen these processes.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DescribeTheMeasuresTakenByTheE:
The Company’s commitment towards safeguarding people, resources and the environment is embedded in the Corporate EHS Policy and is actioned through systems and processes under the EHS Management System (EHSMS). The EHSMS, which is based on and certified against ISO 45001:2018, is implemented at each business level, covering all work locations of the Company. Audit and certification of the EHSMS by independent third-party agencies helps in the identification of areas for further improvement and in maintaining a high standard. Internal audits, conducted at multiple levels, further help strengthen the process.
As part of the EHS Management System, each work location develops a location-specific EHS plan to proactively manage and mitigate risks. While the implementation of these systems is the responsibility of all individuals at the location, key teams such as EHS, HR, Admin and Medical services are central to ensuring compliance with safety and health requirements.
The Company has digital systems and applications to reduce manual efforts in tracking observations, monitoring performance and data analytics. Technologies such as AR/VR are also leveraged to enhance training and safety awareness for employees and workers.
The Company has revamped its EHS strategy and is now focusing on “VISION KNOW HARM”. It combines knowledge and action to ensure effective implementation of SOPs and procedures to help deliver safety performance targets. For details on the new strategy and approach towards safety, refer to the ‘Health, Safety and Well-being’ subsection under the ‘Human Capital’ section of the Integrated Annual Report FY 2025–26.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfAnyCorrectiveActionTa:
The EHS Council of the Company is the apex committee responsible for monitoring the Company’s health and safety performance, identifying areas for continuous improvement and formulating strategies and actions to drive improvements. A dedicated Corporate EHS team, led by a senior professional with extensive industry experience, has been established to identify gaps, define new strategies and drive implementation across businesses. In addition to the actions taken in FY 2024–25, namely the setting up of a Serious Incident Review Committee, institution of a Reward and Penalty system, identification system for new workers and a revamped onboarding process for contractual workers, the Company has taken further steps for improvement.
1. VISION KNOW HARM: Revamped EHS strategy focused on driving knowledge of hazards and risks and actions to prevent any incident. To support this, a BACK-TO-BASICS campaign is being driven across all work locations, reinforcing fundamental safety principles that form the foundation of a strong EHS culture.
2. Occurrence Reporting & Investigation Standard: An organization-wide EHS reporting and investigation standard has been introduced based on Occurrence Root Cause Analysis (ORCA) technique. The standard redefines event and reporting categorization, level of investigation and event notification structure. Training sessions are being conducted for employees to build awareness of the new investigation technique.
3. Accident Investigation Committee (AIC): A committee consisting of the business head in which the serious incident has occurred, along with one independent Root Cause Analysis (RCA) facilitator, has been formed for investigation of serious incidents resulting in fatality or permanent total disablement. The AIC conducts the investigation and presents its findings to the Serious Incident Review Committee (SIRC). Based on the findings of the AIC, the SIRC recommends appropriate disciplinary or corrective actions.
4. EHS Leaders Workshop: A three-day workshop for EHS heads of all the businesses of the Company was organized to formulate actions implementing the new strategy and transformation of EHS culture across the organization.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NotesPrinciple3ExplanatoryText:
ESSENTIAL INDICATORS:-
Question 1 a:
The Company does not have a paternity leave policy.
Data is based on the coverage of creche/day care facility available to the employees in a particular location and not as per usage/availing of creche facility. In case creche facility is not available, creche allowance in provided to female employees (permanent and non-permanent) and permanent female workers.
Question 1 c:
The Company is dedicated to ensuring the comprehensive well-being of its employees and workers through a blend of statutory and voluntary welfare measures. These initiatives focus on enhancing physical health, mental and emotional well-being, financial security, work-life balance and opportunities for professional development. Expenditure related to protective gear and safety equipment are not itemised separately, as these are part of any activity or operations that are carried out. This expenditure is included within indirect or directs costs booked for any contract or business.
The table below presents the total cost incurred on employee well-being measures, including measures such as medical and health insurance, medical benefits, maternity benefits, staff welfare, worker compensation and canteen expenses.
Question 3:
The Company promotes an inclusive and accessible workplace in line with the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016. Most of the premises include ramps, dedicated parking spaces and elevators with accessibility features to support differently abled people.
Question 8:
the number of trainings conducted are higher than the number of employees and workers due to the attrition and new joiners.
Quesion 10 c:
As part of the EHS Management System, the Company has implemented various measures to enable the participation and consultation of workers in hazard identification and risk management. In addition to their involvement in the hazard identification processes mentioned above, other key processes are as follows:
a) Take 5 process: Workers are encouraged to follow five steps: Stop, Look, Identify, Discuss and Control before starting any work. ‘KNOW HARM’ boards support workers in identifying hazards and controlling risks.
b) Pre-Start Verification and toolbox talks: These are conducted before the commencement of each workday to ensure that risks associated with activities are proactively assessed and that operating conditions are compliant with risk assessments and Safe Work methods. Workers are empowered to pause work and report to the concerned officials in case of immediate danger or potential risks. Reporting of hazards is encouraged through spot recognition and periodic safety awards for the correct use of safety protocols. The Site EHS Committee at each location comprises key stakeholders, including worker representatives. The meetings serve as a platform for participation and consultation on all EHS-related matters. Feedback from workers is collated in a structured manner, discussed in safety committee meetings and corrective actions are undertaken, as required.
Question 10 d:
In addition of ensuring a safe working environment, the Company is committed to ensuring the health and wellbeing of workforce, including contractual employees and workers, through comprehensive medical services and well-being programs. The Company has implemented various initiatives to address both occupational and nonoccupational health needs: a) First Aid and Emergency Services: Available for any person working at the location b) Medical Centres: Occupational Health Centres or medical centres assist in managing non-occupational medical cases in addition to providing occupational health services. c) Tie-ups with Local Hospitals: Location-specific agreements with nearby hospitals and nursing homes ensure prioritised access to medical facilities. d) Mental Health and Non-Occupational Health programmes: Annual health-checkups, special health check-up drives (e.g., blood sugar, cardiac health, cancer), vaccination drives (e.g., flu, pneumococcal, cervical) aid in early detection of any issues. Campaigns such as Freedom from Diabetes and health advisories from medical teams help in raising awareness of various health topics.
Question 11:
The Company had undertaken several corrective actions in FY 2024–25 and has also recently revamped its EHS strategy. However, during FY 2025–26, there were still a significant number of fatal accidents. The Company sincerely regrets the loss suffered by the families of the persons affected and has treated this as a critical issue requiring immediate attention. A thorough investigation was conducted into all incidents. To address the shortfalls in processes and enhance the systems, The Company has undertaken further measures to strengthen the implementation of SOPs, as elaborated in Question 15 of the Essential Indicators under Principle 3.
LEADERSHIP INDICATORS:-
Question 1:
The Company is committed to safeguarding the financial well-being of its employees and workers through comprehensive insurance coverage. 1) Life Insurance: All employees are covered under a term life insurance policy arranged by the Company, offering financial protection to their dependents in the event of an untimely demise. 2) Health Insurance (Mediclaim): To cover medical expenses, employees and eligible workers are enrolled under a Mediclaim policy providing health insurance benefits. 3) Non-Permanent Worker: In the unfortunate event of a fatality involving contractual worker, the Company ensures compensatory payments are made in line with applicable laws and regulations. Additional support measures are also be extended on a case-by-case basis, reflecting the Company’s commitment to worker welfare. The Company regularly reviews and updates its insurance coverage and compensatory practices to align with evolving business needs and regulatory frameworks.
Question 4:
The Company provides transition assistance programmes to facilitate continued employability and the management of career endings resulting from retirement on merit.
Question 5:
In line with the Company’s commitment to promoting sustainability across the value chain, the Company had identified and assessed 289 critical supply chain partners, contributing to ~22% by value of business of the Company.
These supply chain partners were assessed under five modules: Governance, Ethical business practices, Human rights and labour management, Health and safety and Environment. The assessment was conducted by an independent third party using a detailed questionnaire aligned with regulatory requirements, BRSR disclosures and global sustainability standards. It was carried out remotely through document review and supplier interactions, as well as through onsite visits for selected low-scoring supply chain partners. Post-assessment, gaps were shared with suppliers along with recommendations and action plans to improve sustainability performance and compliance.
For details of the Company’s initiatives on incorporating sustainability in supply chain, refer to ‘Social and Relationship Capital’ and ‘Human Capital’ for disclosures related to human rights in supply chain of the Integrated Annual Report FY 2025-26.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfMeasuresUndertakenByT: The Company ensures that statutory dues from the value chain partners i.e., subcontractors and supply chain partners, are deposited on time. Proof of payment or record of statutory dues paid by the subcontractors e.g., records for Provident Fund (PF) deposit for contractual workers is maintained. GST payment by the suppliers is matched through GST portal to ensure compliance, amongst other controls.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfAnyCorrectiveActionTa: No significant risks or concerns were identified from the ESG assessments conducted during the year. The Company ensures that all supply chain partners understand and formally acknowledge the Company’s Code of Conduct, which is a mandatory requirement during the vendor registration and onboarding process. To further strengthen ESG awareness and compliance, training sessions were conducted for supply chain partners during the year, covering key Company policies including the Sustainable Supply Chain Policy, Sustainability Policy, Whistleblower Policy and Code of Conduct. In instances where concerns, observations, or potential risks are identified — whether during formal assessments or through other interactions — the Company promptly initiates appropriate corrective and preventive actions to address them.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DescribeTheProcessesForIdentif:
L&T identifies its key stakeholder groups through a structured process aimed at balancing business objectives with stakeholder needs, expectations and ESG considerations to support long-term value creation. Identification is based on factors such as the stakeholder’s influence on the business, their level of dependence on or impact from L&T’s operations and their relevance in the context of evolving ESG and regulatory requirements. The process includes:
.Defining scope and purpose: Establishing whether stakeholder identification is for ESG reporting, materiality assessment, strategy development, or project-level engagement and defining the applicable boundary (enterprise, regional, or project level). .Value chain mapping: Identifying all entities directly or indirectly connected across operations, including suppliers, contractors, customers and other partners. .Stakeholder categorisation: Grouping stakeholders into internal (employees, senior management, Board) and external (customers, investors, regulators, suppliers, communities, NGOs, media, academia) categories. .Assessment of influence and impact: Evaluating stakeholders based on dependency, responsibility, sphere of influence and diversity of perspectives to ensure comprehensive coverage of relevant groups. . ESG and regulatory alignment: Ensuring consistency with applicable sustainability frameworks and emerging regulatory expectations. .Stakeholder feedback integration: Validating and refining stakeholder groups through engagement mechanisms such as surveys, workshops, interviews and grievance channels. .Ongoing review: Periodically updating stakeholder mapping to reflect changes in business operations, market dynamics and external environment.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:ProvideTheProcessesForConsulta:
L&T has established a comprehensive and structured governance framework to enable effective consultation with stakeholders and Board oversight on economic, environmental and social matters. Sustainability strategy, performance and stakeholder considerations are overseen at the Board level through dedicated committees, including the CSR & Sustainability Committee, Risk Management Committee and Stakeholders’ Relationship Committee, each chaired by an Independent Director.
At the management level, specialized committees such as the EHS Council, Material Council and Quality Council focus on specific sustainability dimensions within their defined mandates. These Committees operate under approved terms of reference and meet periodically to review performance, assess risks and opportunities and monitor progress against targets in their respective areas.
Inputs from stakeholder interactions, performance reviews and issue assessments deliberated at the management and Board committee levels including insights on material economic, environmental and social topics are systematically consolidated and escalated to the Board through structured reporting mechanisms. These matters are reviewed during regular Board and committee meetings, including quarterly updates, to enable informed decision making and strategic oversight.
In addition, the Company periodically undertakes structured stakeholder engagement processes on ESG related topics, involving key stakeholder groups such as employees, customers, suppliers, communities, investors and regulators. These engagements follow a defined methodology with prescribed frequency, roles and reporting protocols. Feedback, concerns and expectations emerging from these consultations are formally documented, reviewed by management and presented to the relevant Board committees or the Board, as appropriate. This integrated consultation and escalation framework ensures that stakeholder perspectives are systematically incorporated into governance processes, supports transparency and accountability and strengthens alignment between stakeholder expectations, sustainability priorities and the Company’s strategic direction.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfInstancesAsToHowTheIn:
Environmental topics:
Climate risk assessment: Stakeholders are increasingly demanding greater focus on and disclosure of climate-related risks. In FY 2025–26, a detailed climate risk assessment was conducted with third-party support for AMNHEC-Hazira and Kattupalli, the Company’s two largest manufacturing complex supporting key businesses including
Heavy Engineering, L&T Energy Hydrocarbon-Offshore, Precision Engineering & Systems and L&T Energy Carbon Lite Solutions. . Adaptation and mitigation actions have been identified and will be taken up for implementation. • Waste management and circularity: . I ncreased focus on waste, including plastic waste, in response to regulatory and stakeholder expectations. . L&T Rubber Processing Machinery became the first facility within the Company to achieve zero waste to landfill (ZWL) certification; a few other facilities have also initiated the process for reaching ZWL status. .AMNHEC and Knowledge City Vadodara have been certified as single-use plastic-free campuses by Confederation of Indian Industry.
Energy management: .Various external stakeholders such as customers and rating agencies sought details on energy management systems (EnMS) implementation by the Company. ISO 50001 certification received for multiple businesses including for EPC projects businesses; wider EnMS implementation is ongoing across EPC projects sites and manufacturing facilities. . Structured, technology-led approach implemented, including a diesel reduction task force and Corporate Energy Management Cell.
Social Topics: Safety concerns resulted in stronger focus on preventive OHS measures, training, and leading indicators. Feedback on well-being and gender issues has been incorporated through enhanced employee well-being initiatives and strengthened DEI efforts.
POSH policy: .Now expanded to be gender-neutral; which was earlier limited only to female .Applicable to L&T globally; which was earlier limited to establishments in India .Online portal, hosted by a third-party, launched to enable proper tracking, escalation and resolution of complaints • Occupational safety: Refreshed approach introduced in FY 2025–26 anchored in two objectives . ‘KNOW HARM’ focus on hazard identification and risk management. . ‘No Harm’ focus on prevention of harm to people, assets and environment.
Increased focus on Mental health and well-being .100 employees trained as Mental Health First Aiders to identify and support mental health concerns.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfInstancesOfEngagement:
Vulnerable Group: Farmer community in water stressed and drought prone locations
Concerns: Drought, Poverty, Migration
Actions Taken: Organised farmers community to form Village; Development Committees (VDC) and Farmers Producer Organisation (FPO) ; Water made available with wastewater and conservation intervention ;Capacity building in sustainable agricultural practices ; Maintenance of waterstructures by the VDC along with Panchayat
Impact: Rise in ground water table and water made available to population of 1,06,008 from 11 ICDP locations and 8,411 hectares land protected from direct run-off in three ICDP locations; Increase in household agricultural income; Reverse migration
Vulnerable Group: Rural population without access to proper sanitation facilities
Concerns: Open defecation leading to health issues and social disgrace
Actions Taken: Community awareness regarding making villages open defecation free (ODF); Construction of toilets after ensuring water availability; Village level monitoring committee formed to ensure ODF status of the village
Impact: 5,366 toilets constructed and 41 villages are made ODF; Women felt safe to use household toilet and saved from embarrassment and social disgrace
Vulnerable Group: Underprivileged community from urban and peri-urban areas
Concerns: Unaffordable and inaccessible health services; Patients cannot afford the dialysis treatment in private hospitals; Unskilled-Unemployed youth: Unemployed youth trained in L&T’s Construction Skills Training Institutes and facilitation for job placements
Actions Taken: Affordable general health services along with consultations in specialised clinics provided through nine health centres across India and mobile health vans; Provision of dialysis services at a concessional rate; Unemployed youth trained in L&T’s Construction Skills Training Institutes and facilitation for job placements
Impact: 10,09,069 services dispensed to underprivileged population; Dialysis patients taking regular treatment at the L&T health centre; Around 10,000 youth trained every year of which 60-70% get job placements
Vulnerable group: Students in tribal/ rural schools or urban resource poor schools
Concern: Students have difficulties in learning science and maths subjects and no access to digital and hands on education
Action Taken: Providing Science, Technology, Engineering and Math (STEM) Kits for hands on learning in science and math and digital infrastructure, digital content mapped to the curriculum and training to teachers to conduct classes using digital media
Impact: 5,292 students took part inscience exhibitions; Students making their own STEM models and presenting solutions to everyday problem by using technology
Vulnerable Group: School students without access to clean toilets
Concerns: School Toilets dilapidated or not functional
Actions Taken: Constructed toilet blocks inthe schools for students
Impacts: Increase in attendance of girls

xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DescribeTheInternalMechanismsI:
Respect for and commitment to human rights are fundamental components of the Company’s Code of Conduct for employees. Any violations for clauses under Code of conduct are reported to the first-level reporting authority, who investigates and takes appropriate action. If the violation involves the first-level authority, it is escalated to the second level. Severe cases are referred to the Whistleblower Investigation Committee for action within a reasonable timeframe.
The Company is committed to a safe and inclusive workplace free from sexual harassment. A Policy for the Protection of Women’s Rights at the Workplace has been established in line with the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013 (POSH Act). This applies to all employees and workers of the Company and to visitors including customers, contractors, vendors, supply chain partners and business representatives globally regardless of gender. Where sexual harassment involves third parties, the Company takes reasonable steps to support affected individuals. Internal Complaints Committees (ICCs) have been set up across administrative units to register complaints, conduct investigations, conclude proceedings and recommend action. ICC recommendations are implemented by the Company. The ICCs also conduct regular awareness sessions and workshops. Two Apex Committees at the senior level, with representatives from ICCs and leadership, oversee POSH implementation and compliance across the Company.
The Company also has a Whistleblower mechanism that encourages employees and vendors to report unethical behaviour, misconduct, legal or regulatory violations or unfair practices without fear of retaliation. All complaints are investigated and appropriate corrective actions are taken.
For details on the governance and management processes related to Human Rights, refer to ‘Human Capital’ chapter of the Integrated Annual Report FY2024-25.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:RemarksforComplaintsExplanator: Complaints registered and redressed under the POSH Act
Complaints registered and redressed under the POSH Act
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:MechanismsToPreventAdverseCons: The mechanism remains as detailed in the answer to Question 5. The Company has a Code of Conduct applicable to all employees, senior management and Board members, outlining expected standards of ethical behaviour and professional conduct. All violations are to be reported as per the reporting matrix defined in the policy framework. The Code also sets out the grievance redressal process and specifies preventive and corrective actions to ensure compliance with ethical business practices.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfAnyCorrectiveActionsT: No significant risks/concerns arose that required any corrective actions with respect to human rights related issues.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfABusinessProcessBeing: The Company’s Code of Conduct for its employees as well as supply chain partners and Sustainable Supply chain policy covers its commitment to human rights. No complaints have been received through the channels mentioned above; the details have already been discussed in Section A ‘VII. Transparency and disclosures compliances.’
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfTheScopeAndCoverageOf: The Company’s human rights due diligence framework covers the contractual worker and encompasses its own operational footprint, including EPC project sites, manufacturing facilities and offices. This evaluates compliance with human rights principles, focusing on the prevention of child labour, elimination of forced or involuntary labour, payment of fair and timely wages, prevention of sexual harassment, eradication of modern slavery practices, promotion of non-discrimination, assurance of safe and healthy working conditions and provision of accessible grievance redressal mechanisms.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfAnyCorrectiveActionsT: No significant human rights risks or concerns were identified during the reporting year. All supply chain partners are required to be aware of and formally agree to the Company’s Code of Conduct as part of vendor registration and onboarding. If any risks or concerns are identified during the year, through assessments or otherwise, the Company takes appropriate corrective and preventive actions to address and mitigate them.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NotesPrinciple5ExplanatoryText:
ESSENTIAL INDICATORS:-
Question 1:
All new joiners undergo compliance training that includ Code of Conduct and human rights to maintain the training coverage. The Company has a dedicated human rights training module on its learning management system for its employees, supplemented by awareness sessions for workers. Induction and toolbox talk for all site workers cover wage details, PF deductions, health and safety, banking and KYC processes. Compliance systems are in place to prevent child and forced labour, including age verification and employment documentation. Daily toolbox talks also reinforce these aspects along with job-specific guidance.
Key Company locations at Hazira and Kattupalli are certified under SA 8000 by independent third parties, covering child labour, forced labour, discrimination, working hours, remuneration, freedom of association and grievance mechanisms. Refer to the Human Capital section of the Integrated Annual Report FY 2025–26 for details.
Question 3 b:
In FY 2024–25 and FY 2025–26, wages reported are for employees (both permanent and non-permanent) and permanent workers. Given that the Company has computed the values based on CTC, this includes retirement benefits as well.
The Company engages workers through service and manpower contracts, with service contracts accounting for over 70% of engagements. For manpower contracts, the Company has direct oversight of deployed workers. However, for service contracts, wages are not directly monitored as they are paid by the contractor, but statutory compliance is ensured. In some cases, subcontractors obtain labour licenses and file returns under the Contract Labour (Regulation and Abolition) Act (CLRA) independently. Consequently, such data may not fully reflect in CLRA reporting.
FY 2025–26 data is based on CLRA filings for calendar year 2025 (January to December), covering both contract categories. Data collation and reporting is largely manual and there are gaps in the supporting evidence submitted by contractors to substantiate the figures reported in the Company’s CLRA filings. Furthermore, since data on wages is primarily available on a calendar-year basis, it does not align with the financial year reporting period, posing challenges in reconciliation. The Company is strengthening wage reporting systems to improve accuracy and alignment.
Question 4:
The Chief Human Resources Officer is the focal point for human rights related issues at the Company level. For implementation across the sites and manufacturing plants, designated personnel from IR/Admin/EHS functions are responsible for human rights and labour management. At business level, IR/Admin Heads of respective businesses are the focal point supported by HR heads.
Question 7:
"Complaints on POSH as a % of female employees / workers" Covers and includes females based in India, both employee and worker (permanent and other than permanent.) categories.
Under "Complaints on POSH upheld", ‘upheld’ is interpreted by the Company as the complaints concluded and redressed.
Question 9:
human rights requirements are integrated into business contracts. The Company’s Supplier Code of Conduct covers human rights, labour standards and societal well-being, aligned with UN Global Compact Principles and ILO Core Conventions. All supply chain partners are required to acknowledge and comply with this Code and signing it is mandatory during vendor onboarding. The Code includes requirements on fair and safe working conditions, occupational health and safety, prohibition of child and forced labour, non-discrimination, zero tolerance for harassment and ethical business practices. Contracts also include compliance with applicable laws such as wage payments, Provident Fund (PF) deductions and other statutory labour obligations. Additionally, compliance with applicable regulatory requirements — including health and quality standards, statutory wage payments, PF deductions and other labour-related obligations — is formally integrated into all supplier agreements and contractual obligations.
Question 10:
Key manufacturing plants are certified under SA 8000, a globally recognised standard covering human rights and labour management. In addition, an annual internal due diligence through a structured self-assessment questionnaire is conducted across manufacturing plants, offices and project sites to identify potential human rights risks, including health and safety systems and working conditions. This process involves cross-functional teams from Administration, Industrial Relations (IR), Projects, HR and EHS.
LEADERSHIP INDICATORS:-
Question 4:
In line with the Company’s commitment to promoting sustainability across the value chain, the Company had identified and assessed 289 critical supply chain partners, contributing to ~22% by value of business of the Company.
These supply chain partners were assessed under five modules: Governance, Ethical business practices, Human rights and labour management, Health and safety and Environment. The assessment was conducted by an independent third party using a detailed questionnaire aligned with regulatory requirements, BRSR disclosures and global sustainability standards. It was carried out remotely through document review and supplier interactions, as well as through onsite visits for selected low-scoring supply chain partners. Post-assessment, gaps were shared with supply chain partners along with recommendations and action plans to improve sustainability performance and compliance.
For details of the Company’s initiatives on incorporating sustainability in supply chain, refer to ‘Social and Relationship Capital’ and ‘Human Capital’ for disclosures related to human rights in supply chain of the Integrated Annual Report FY 2025-26.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NameOfTheExternalAgencyIfAnyIn: Reasonable level of data assurance has been carried out by BDO India Services Private Limited. The assurance statement is provided at the end of BRSR section in Integrated Annual Report FY 2025-26.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NameOfTheExternalAgencyInCaseA: Reasonable level of data assurance has been carried out by BDO India Services Private Limited. The assurance statement is provided at the end of BRSR section in Integrated Annual Report FY 2025-26.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NameOfTheExternalAgencyThatUnd: Reasonable level of data assurance has been carried out by BDO India Services Private Limited. The assurance statement is provided at the end of BRSR section in Integrated Annual Report FY 2025-26.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfCoverageAndImplementa: Major facilities at Hazira, Kattupalli, Talegaon, Coimbatore, Ranoli, Kancheepuram and Kansbahal have implemented Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD) systems. These ensure that 100% of wastewater generated from operations is treated and either recycled or reused. Treated water is used for non-potable purposes such as gardening, toilet flushing, firefighting, road washing and dust suppression, reducing freshwater consumption and environmental impact.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NameOfTheExternalAgencyIfAnyIn: Yes, emissions from chimney stacks at respective manufacturing facilities of the Company are verified by government approved laboratories and reports are submitted to State Pollution Control Boards. Results are reviewed and analysed by the business units at the respective locations to ensure compliance to the CTO.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NameOfTheExternalAgencyThatUnd: Yes, reasonable level of data assurance has been carried out by BDO India Services Private Limited. The assurance statement is provided at the end of BRSR section in Integrated Annual Report FY 2025-26.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfProjectRelatedToReduc: Details of a few initiatives taken for GHG emissions reduction has been disclosed under Leadership Indicator Question 4 of Principle-6.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NameOfTheExternalAgencyThatUnd: Reasonable level of data assurance has been carried out by BDO India Services Private Limited. The assurance statement is provided at the end of BRSR section in Integrated Annual Report FY 2025-26.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfWasteManagementPracti:
Waste management is part of the Company’s Environment and EHS Management Systems across all locations. Each EPC site, manufacturing facility and campus follows a location-specific waste management plan based on waste type, quantity and disposal requirements. The focus is on the 3Rs - Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, to minimise waste generation and disposal.
Hazardous waste, where generated, is handled in line with Central or State Pollution Control Boards (CPCB/SPCB) consents and applicable regulations, including the Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Amendment Rules, 2025 and is disposed only through authorised agencies. Training and awareness are provided to employees and workers.
The Company’s product portfolio, comprising complex, engineered-to-order equipment for process industries and other sectors, does not involve hazardous or toxic chemicals, further reducing risk. Refer to the Natural Capital section of the Integrated Annual Report FY 2025–26 for details.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:LocationOfOperationsOrOffices: A. M. Naik Heavy Engineering Complex, Hazira, Gujarat – the Company’s largest manufacturing complex spread over 750 acres with a 1.6 km waterfront facility. The complex is located along the banks of Tapi River, 8 kms from the Arabian Sea. Only two jetty areas fall under Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) and not the entire facility.

Manufacturing facilities in Kattupalli - Modular Fabrication Facility and Shipbuilding Facility: The facilities are located in Kattupalli (~50 kms from Chennai, Tamil Nadu) along the Coromandel Coast adjoining the Bay of Bengal. Only a part of the facility falls under CRZ and not the entire facility. Manufacturing facility Yes 3 Kachchi Dargah-Bidupur Bridge, Bihar – EPC
Kachchi Dargah-Bidupur Bridge, Bihar – EPC project contract for construction of a 6-lane road bridge which spans the Ganges, connecting Kachchi Dargah in Patna and Bidupur in Hajipur.

Mumbai-Ahmedabad High-Speed Rail C-3 Package (between Shilphata and Zaroli village near Maharashtra-Gujarat border) – EPC project contract for construction of high-speed rail corridor, comprising of some viaducts and tunnels falling in forest area and CRZ.

Orange Gate to Coastal Road, Maharashtra – EPC project contract for construction of underground tunnel road from the Eastern freeway to Netaji Subhash Chandra Road near Marine drive in Mumbai. Some portion of the road falls under CRZ.

Dahisar Bhayander Bridge project, Maharashtra – EPC project contracts (2 packages) related to construction of 26.3 km, eight-lane elevated northern extension of Mumbai’s coastal road network, connecting Versova to Bhayandar. Several portions of the road fall under CRZ boundaries.

Amaala Utilities, Saudi Arabia – EPC contract for construction of renewable energy system comprising of 250MWp solar PV plant and 700MWp battery energy storage system. Located within Prince Mohammed bin Salman Royal Reserve.
Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum Solar Park Phase IV – EPC contract for construction of 1800 MWp solar PV plant. Located inside Al Marmoom Desert Conservation Reserve in UAE.

Sazagan 1 and 2, Uzbekistan – EPC contract for construction of renewable energy system comprising 1GW solar PV plant, 668 MW battery energy storage system and transmission lines. Located near Mubarek State Wildlife Sanctuary.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DetailsOfSignificantDirectAndI:
1. Location of operations/offices : A. M. Naik Heavy Engineering Complex, Hazira (Only two jetty areas fall under CRZ and not the entire facility) Actions taken : No painting or sandblasting is carried out in designated areas and no solid or liquid waste is disposed of in coastal zones. A Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD) system is in place and green cover with native species has been developed. All applicable CRZ regulations are being complied with.
2. Location of operations/offices : Manufacturing facilities in Kattupalli; Only a portion of the facility falls within the CRZ area; the entire facility is not covered under CRZ. Actions taken : No ship-breaking activities are undertaken and oil spill mitigation measures are in place. A Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD) system has been implemented and phosphating and galvanizing activities are conducted beyond CRZ boundaries. All applicable CRZ regulations are being complied with.
3. Location of operations/offices : Kachchi Dargah-Bidupur Bridge, Bihar (EPC project contract) Actions taken : An Environment Management Plan, monitored by a third-party agency, is in place. Silt barriers have been installed to prevent soil erosion near the banks of the Ganges. Wastewater from construction and domestic use is treated through STPs, with no discharge into the river. Muck is disposed of as per norms after testing for hazardous content. Construction is minimized during nighttime and key wildlife or breeding seasons. DG sets used for backup power are fitted with acoustic enclosures.
4. Location of operations/offices : Mumbai-Ahmedabad High-Speed Rail C-3 Package, Maharashtra (EPC project contract) Actions taken : An Environment Management Plan, monitored by a third-party agency, is in place. Noise and vibration levels are monitored and acoustic enclosures or noise barriers installed for high-noise generating equipment. Limited type of activities allowed during nighttime. Controlled lighting has been implemented at yards and equipment movement is monitored to mitigate negative impact on fauna. Special arrangements made for disposal of muck and construction waste. Recycling of wastewater has been implemented and disposal, if any, through authorized agencies.
5. Location of operations/offices : Orange Gate to Coastal Road, Maharashtra (EPC project contract) Actions taken : Environmental impact is assessed as minimal, based on the customer’s EIA study. Trucks and dumpers are covered during material transport and construction waste disposal. Dust suppression measures are implemented across the site and acoustic enclosures and noise barriers are installed for high-noise equipment and activities.
6. Location of operations/offices : Dahisar Bhayander Bridge project, Maharashtra (EPC project contract) Actions taken : An Environment Management Plan, monitored by a third-party agency, is in place. Acoustic enclosures and noise barriers are installed for high-noise equipment and nighttime activities are limited. Dust suppression measures are implemented across the site and proper waste management ensures no discharge of waste or wastewater into creeks and mudflats.
7. Location of operations/offices : Amaala Utilities, Saudi Arabia (EPC project contract) Actions taken : Suitable plants and shrubs have been translocated in line with Amaala’s procedures. Topsoil from vegetated areas is stripped and stored safely for reuse. Offshore works, where applicable, are conducted in the presence of a Marine Mammal Observer. Archaeological structures are protected with barricading and signboards.
8. Location of operations/offices : Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum Solar Park Phase VI (EPC project contract) Actions taken : All activities are confined within the pre-defined designated construction corridor. A 30-metre no-work exclusion buffer is established around identified sensitive habitats, including active burrows. Translocation activities, approved by the Dubai Municipality, are completed prior to construction. Barriers are installed to restrict animal movement and reduce disturbance. Any presence of biodiversity is reported to the authorities in line with the Biodiversity Management Plan.
9. Location of operations/offices : Sazagan 1 and 2, Uzbekistan (EPC project contract) Actions taken : Silt trap curtains have been installed for earthworks near unprotected canals, streams and rivers. Excavated topsoil is segregated and stored for site restoration of laydown areas and buffer zones. Equipment and vehicle washing is carried out in designated areas with impermeable surfaces and isolated drainage systems. Noise control measures e.g barriers and acoustic enclosures, are implemented at identified hotspots. Biodiversity Chance Find Procedures and a Tortoise Translocation Plan for Central Asian tortoise have been implemented.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DisclosureWebLinkOfEntityAtWhi: Incorporating resilience in operations is critical to ensure business continuity. The Company has a comprehensive business continuity and disaster management framework in place across all work locations to ensure that any significant risk emanating from disasters are properly managed. • Site-specific emergency response plans are developed based on local conditions and risks and potential emergencies covering natural and manmade events such as fires, chemical releases, accidents and disease outbreaks. These plans define response procedures, communication and escalation protocols, roles of personnel including the designated emergency response team at the location, emergency facilities as well as coordination with the other public and private agencies relevant to the situation e.g. local public fire department, contact details of personnels from other private companies in the vicinity. • Training and communication are key to ensuring effective implementation of the plan and the workforce, including the contractual workers, are made aware of SOPs relevant to disaster management. • Audits and mock drills are also conducted periodically to check effectiveness of the plans. • A centralized Crisis Management SOP addresses emerging geopolitical and cybersecurity risks, focusing on safeguarding personnel and assets while enabling swift, coordinated response to minimize operational disruptions and ensure continuity.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:DiscloseAnySignificantAdverseI: No significant risks or concerns were raised during the year. The Company ensures that contractors and supply chain partners adhere to its policies and guidelines, including compliance with applicable environmental regulations and responsible business practices.

Question 12:
For EPC project site, the responsibility for conducting Environmental Impact Assessments (EIA) lies with the customer. The Company executes projects in line with the environmental clearances obtained by the customer, as per contractual scope. While EIA is not within the Company’s scope, it is compliant with environmental norms and committed to undertaking good practices during execution. All site activities align with applicable regulations and the Company’s Environment Management System, ensuring mitigation of environmental risks and adherence to sustainability standards.
LEADERSHIP INDICATORS:-
Question 1:
Volume of water for water stress areas has increased compared to previous year due to increase in activities at the EPC project sites located in water stress area and enhancement in data capturing of water data for water stress areas. The Company implements watershed development projects in water stressed areas, as a part of CSR interventions, to augment the water availability.
Question 7: 289 critical supply chain partners were assessed during the year, which is 22% of the total procurement by value of the Company in FY 2025-26.
Question 8:
How many Green Credits have been generated or procured:
A. By the listed entity. No Green Credits have been generated or procured by the Company in FY 2025-26. Green Credits programme of Ministry of Environment, Forests and Climate Change (MoEFCC) does not currently allow private companies to participate in the programme.
B. By the top ten (in terms of value of purchases and sales, respectively) value chain partners. A few value chain partners, which are public sector enterprises, had applied and paid for procurement of Green Credits. However, no Green Credits have been credited to their account in FY 2025-26. Green Credits programme of Ministry of Environment, Forests and Climate Change (MoEFCC) does not currently allow private companies to participate in the programme.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:InitiativeUndertaken: Switching from DG sets to grid power
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:WebLinkForDetailsOfInitiativeT: DG sets have traditionally been the primary power source at EPC sites. To reduce diesel use, the Company is securing grid power connections across locations. In FY 2025–26, key progress was made in Heavy Civil Infrastructure, Water & Effluent Treatment and Transport Infrastructure projects. This shift lowers diesel consumption, reduces emissions and improves operational efficiency.

In FY 2025–26, renewable electricity sourcing increased significantly across multiple sites, including manufacturing facilities at Kansbahal, Kancheepuram, Kattupalli and Talegaon and a few EPC project sites. This was enabled through a mix of on-site solar installations, Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) and green tariff procurement.

Reducing freshwater withdrawal is a key focus area to achieve water neutrality. The Company is scaling up wastewater recycling through sewage treatment plants across all locations. Recycled water is used for non-potable purposes such as landscaping, toilet flushing, road washing and dust suppression.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:OutcomeOfTheInitiative: Freshwater avoided, through wastewater recycling, for FY 2025-26: ~1.15 lakh kL
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:WebLinkForDetailsOfInitiativeT: Concrete equipment such as pumps and pipelines require priming for safe and efficient operation. Traditionally, cement slurry is used. The Company is adopting polymer-based slurry at select EPC sites, which needs minimal on-site preparation and reduces water and resource use.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:OutcomeOfTheInitiative: Freshwater reduction by more than 80% per cu. mtr. of slurry to be used for priming
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:WebLinkForDetailsOfInitiativeT: Wood is not a major input material; however, packaging of engineering goods, especially solar panels in the Renewables business, generates notable wood waste. At select EPC sites, such as Al Khushyabi (Saudi Arabia), this waste is being recycled into usable on-site products, including barricades, signage, pedestrian walkways, fire extinguisher boxes, bulletin boards and storage boxes.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:OutcomeOfTheInitiative: Helped avoid ~151 MT of wood waste going to the landfill.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NameOfTheArea: Water-stressed blocks or areas in Gujarat, Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh, Haryana, Madhya Pradesh, National Capital Territory of Delhi in India, and in Kingdom of Saudi Arabia and United Arab Emirates.
xbrli:xbrl.in-capmkt:NatureOfOperations: Execution of EPC projects’ contracts awarded by customers, related to highways, oil & gas facilities, solar power plants, metro rail, pumped storage and buildings
